text: Precise delivery and high drug loading remain significant hurdles in cancer chemotherapy. While self-assembling drug delivery systems (DSDSs) offer a promising alternative to traditional nanocarriers, maintaining supramolecular stability within the complex physiological environment of a living organism is challenging. Here, we report a light-activated anticancer palladium complex, PdL, that utilizes metallophilic Pd···Pd interactions to drive in vivo self-assembly. Density functional theory and experimental characterization confirm that these interactions facilitate the formation of stable nanostructures. In a human skin melanoma (A375) xenograft model, these self-assembled nanoparticles exhibit an exceptionally long blood circulation time (over 12 hours) and achieve high tumor accumulation (up to 10.2% of the injected dose per gram). Upon activation with green light, the complex triggers a potent type I photodynamic effect, generating superoxide radicals even under hypoxic conditions. This results in significant tumor destruction, evidenced by up to a 96-fold increase in cytotoxicity in vitro and robust anti-tumor efficacy in vivo. This study demonstrates that metallophilic self-assembly provides a powerful strategy for developing stable, high-loading supramolecular nanotherapeutics with superior tumor-targeting capabilities.
